original_abstract: Specific effects of electrolytes have posed a challenge since the 1880's. The pioneering work was that of Franz Hofmeister who studied specific salt induced protein precipitation. These effects are the rule rather the exception and are ubiquitous in chemistry and biology. Conventional electrostatic theories (Debye-Hückel, DLVO, etc.) cannot explain such effects. Over the past decades it has been recognised that additional quantum mechanical dispersion forces with associated hydration effects acting on ions are missing from theory. In parallel Collins has proposed a phenomenological set of rules (the law of matching water affinities, LMWA) which explain and bring to order the order of ion-ion and ion-surface site interactions at a qualitative level. The two approaches appear to conflict. Although the need for inclusion of quantum dispersion forces in one form or another is not questioned, the modelling has often been misleading and inappropriate. It does not properly describe the chemical nature (kosmotropic/chaotropic or hard/soft) of the interacting species. The success of the LMWA rules lies in the fact that they do. Here we point to the way that the two apparently opposing approaches might be reconciled. Notwithstanding, there are more challenges, which deal with the effect of dissolved gas and its connection to 'hydrophobic' interactions, the problem of water at different temperatures and 'water structure' in the presence of solutes. They take us to another dimension that requires the rebuilding of theoretical foundations.
humanized_abstract: For nearly a century and a half it has been generally acknowledged that the effects of particular electrolytes present one of the main unsolved problems of chemistry and of biology. The work of Franz Hofmeister was associated with salt-induced protein precipitation, but special interactions are much more common. It has been well recognised in the last few decades that ions have quantum mechanical dispersion interactions with other ions, and also surfaces and site locations together with associated hydration interactions, are lacking in conventional electrostatic descriptions of interactions. A way of bringing some order into ion–ion, ion–surface and ion–site interactions is by invoking Collins’ Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA) rules that in qualitative terms correctly establishes their order. However, the LMWA and conventional descriptions are clearly not the same and indeed would seem not to be compatible. The inclusion of the omitted quantum dispersion forces in their various guises in modelling electrostatics is generally accepted as necessary but any modelling in this area that has appeared has often been misconceived in terms of the correct description of ion chemistry – that is kosmotropic/chaotropic (hard/soft). It will be argued that the reason for the effectiveness of the LMWA is exactly that they do provide a better description of these ion properties. There is still considerable scope for additional scientific advance here. Other challenges lie ahead for building a theory of the role of dissolved gases, especially for explaining some of the effects that are denoted by the term hydrophobic and understanding aqueous properties of water at different temperatures as well as so-called ‘water structure’ and ‘order’ when modified by solutes.
